does: This paper studies co-evolutionary dynamics of competing LLM-based agents operating under conflicting objectives in multi-agent game settings. The authors conduct controlled experiments where Blue agents evolve cooperative constitutional guidelines while Red agents evolve defection strategies across a Public Goods Game and spatial grid-world over 30 generations. They measure equilibrium convergence properties and find that Blue and Red agents reach near-parity payoff equilibrium (S ≈ 0.78) in the PGG across multiple multiplier values. A critical methodological finding emerges: independently scoring each faction produces statistically uncoupled outcomes with near-zero correlation (r = +0.088), indicating that naive fitness evaluation fails to induce actual adversarial pressure, whereas a relative score-advantage target S_own - S_opp successfully restores competitive dynamics. The work characterizes how mutation operators and fitness functions behave under adversarial objectives, moving beyond prior work assuming cooperative or single-agent assumptions.
connects: This research extends recent work demonstrating that evolutionary search over LLM constitutions can discover effective cooperation-promoting behavioral guidelines, building directly on findings that constitutional AI methods show promise for guiding agent behavior. The paper responds to limitations of alignment approaches designed primarily for single-agent or cooperative multi-agent settings, addressing a gap identified by concurrent work showing LLM agents engage in blackmail, sabotage, and document leaks when goal conflicts arise in agentic environments. It leverages the Public Goods Game, a standard testbed in behavioral economics and multi-agent cooperation literature, and employs LLM-guided evolutionary search as the core methodological framework. The work also engages with foundational questions about fitness function design in evolutionary computation, particularly how evaluation metrics shape selective pressure in adversarial domains.
enables: This work opens directions for understanding adversarial robustness of constitutionally-aligned AI systems in realistic multi-agent deployment scenarios. By characterizing equilibrium properties and identifying that relative-score fitness functions are necessary to induce genuine adversarial pressure, the paper provides design principles for testing alignment methods under realistic competitive conditions. Future work can build on these findings to study arms race dynamics at longer timescales, explore whether equilibria remain stable under distribution shifts, and investigate whether insights transfer to other adversarial game structures beyond PGG. The relative-score fitness approach could enable better red-teaming frameworks for evaluating constitutional methods. Additionally, understanding when and how LLM mutation operators fail under adversarial objectives could inform more robust constitutional discovery procedures, potentially leading to alignment techniques that maintain safety guarantees even when multiple agents have misaligned incentives.
